chapters.title: Present simple vs present continuous
chapters.summary: Use present simple for routines and general truths, and present continuous for actions in progress or temporary situations.
points: Present simple describes habits and general truths.
Present continuous describes actions happening now.
Present continuous can describe temporary situations.

examples.english: She is working from home this week.

pitfalls: Do not overuse present continuous with stative verbs.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write three sentence pairs contrasting present simple and present continuous.

chapters.title: Present simple vs present continuous (2)
chapters.summary: Some stative verbs usually prefer the present simple, but the continuous can sometimes be used to emphasize temporariness or change.
points: Many state verbs are more commonly used in the present simple.
The present continuous can highlight temporary behavior or change.
pitfalls: Not every verb that describes a state sounds natural in the continuous.

practicePrompts.prompt: Rewrite five sentences using the more natural tense.

chapters.title: Applying for a job
chapters.summary: Vocabulary for job ads, cover letters, applications and career-related language.
points: A cover letter should highlight relevant experience and suitability for the role.
Job ads often mention career prospects, training and salary expectations.
Applications should stand out clearly and professionally.

examples.english: Please find attached my CV in support of my application.
The post offers excellent career prospects.

pitfalls: Do not confuse a cover letter with a CV.
Avoid overly casual language in job applications.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write a short cover letter for an entry-level role.
List five phrases commonly used in job advertisements.

chapters.title: Job interviews
chapters.summary: Vocabulary and phrases used in job interviews, including preparation, confidence, first impressions, interview panels and job offers.
points: Candidates may be shortlisted for interview based on selection criteria.
It is important to do your homework before an interview.
Body language and first impressions matter.
Candidates may be asked about previous roles, responsibilities and notice period.
A job offer may include salary, benefits and reporting structure.

examples.english: I was shortlisted for an interview last week.
We believe your qualifications are an ideal fit for the role.
My notice period is two weeks.

pitfalls: Do not confuse a line manager with an interview panel.
Do not use overly casual language when discussing experience in an interview.

practicePrompts.prompt: Write five interview questions and model answers.
Describe a previous role using formal interview language.
Draft a short acceptance email for a job offer.
